concept_ref: §2 Levels 4a/4b — 'Exponential test explosion' vs 'N+1 tests' / §3 row 4

scenario.title: The Cost of Exhaustiveness
scenario.narrative: Before you sign off on a 5-condition safety-critical predicate, your project manager asks for the test budget. Compute the test counts for both BCC and MCDC at N=5.
scenario.code:
if a and b and c and d and e:
    detonate()

claim: Quick budget check before the next case: how many tests does each technique require?

numeric_prompts.question: Number of tests required by BCC for N=5 conditions
Minimum number of tests required by MCDC for N=5 conditions (N+1)

hints: BCC tests every combination of TRUE/FALSE for every condition. For N independent conditions, that is 2^N rows of the truth table.
MCDC needs at most N+1 tests for N conditions (one carefully engineered test per condition for its independence pair, plus a shared baseline). Re-read Law Card 6.

Solution — BCC: 2^5 = 32. MCDC: 5 + 1 = 6. The cost ratio (32/6 ≈ 5.3×) explains why DO-178C aviation software mandates MCDC, not BCC. As N grows, BCC becomes infeasible: at N=10 the ratio is 1024/11 ≈ 93×.

misconceptions.explanation_md: BCC and MCDC have very different test budgets. BCC = 2^N (exponential, infeasible at scale). MCDC = N+1 (linear, feasible). They also differ in *guarantee*: BCC catches every interaction; MCDC catches only those where each condition can independently change the outcome. For safety-critical software, MCDC is the practical sweet spot — strong enough to detect masking bugs, cheap enough to actually run.
